Musical artist

**Gregory Arthur Phillinganes** (born May 12, 1956) is an American keyboardist, vocalist, and arranger. A [session musician](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician"), Phillinganes has contributed to numerous albums over a broad array of artists and genres. He has toured with artists including [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [David Gilmour](/wiki/David_Gilmour "David Gilmour"), [Toto](/wiki/Toto_(band) "Toto (band)") and [The Pussycat Dolls](/wiki/The_Pussycat_Dolls "The Pussycat Dolls"), was musical director for [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson"), and has released two solo studio albums.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greg_Phillinganes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Gregory Arthur Phillinganes was born in Detroit, Michigan, on May 12, 1956 to Elaine and Ardick Phillinganes.[[2]](#cite_note-Wynn-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) He began playing a neighbor's piano by ear at the age of two, beginning lessons a few years later after his mother purchased a piano for him.[[1]](#cite_note-Regen-1) He took lessons from two different instructors, then from Misha Kotler, a [Detroit Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Detroit_Symphony_Orchestra "Detroit Symphony Orchestra") pianist who introduced the discipline and technique Phillinganes required. He credits Kotler with showing him proper hand posture and for influencing him to play with "a sense of dexterity and definition".[[1]](#cite_note-Regen-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greg_Phillinganes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

After drummer [Ricky Lawson](/wiki/Ricky_Lawson "Ricky Lawson") gave [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder") a cassette of Phillinganes' instrumental renditions of his own songs, Phillinganes auditioned for Wonder's band, Wonderlove, and played with them from 1976 to 1981.[[1]](#cite_note-Regen-1)
The year 1978 began a three-decade-long involvement with [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") and [the Jacksons](/wiki/The_Jacksons "The Jacksons") for Phillinganes. He arranged the Jacksons' 1978 album *[Destiny](/wiki/Destiny_(The_Jacksons_album) "Destiny (The Jacksons album)")* and played keyboard on their follow-up album *[Triumph](/wiki/Triumph_(The_Jacksons_album) "Triumph (The Jacksons album)")*.[[4]](#cite_note-atlanta-4) He accompanied the [Jackson family](/wiki/Jackson_family "Jackson family") to [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World "Walt Disney World") and referred to [Katherine Jackson](/wiki/Katherine_Jackson "Katherine Jackson") as "Mom".[[5]](#cite_note-UYS-5)

Phillinganes contributed to Michael's solo albums. He was hired as a session musician for his contributions to Jackson's best-selling *[Thriller](/wiki/Thriller_(Michael_Jackson_album) "Thriller (Michael Jackson album)")*,[[6]](#cite_note-6) but did not receive [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties").[[5]](#cite_note-UYS-5)

He served as the musical director for Jackson's *[Bad](/wiki/Bad_(tour) "Bad (tour)")* and *[Dangerous](/wiki/Dangerous_World_Tour "Dangerous World Tour")* concert tours, and the [Michael Jackson: 30th Anniversary Special](/wiki/Michael_Jackson:_30th_Anniversary_Special "Michael Jackson: 30th Anniversary Special"). In 2022, Phillinganes was mentioned in several episodes of the podcast series *Stories in the Room: Michael Jackson's Thriller Album Podcast*.

In 1981, Phillinganes released his first solo album, *Significant Gains*.[[7]](#cite_note-SG-7) *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* reviewer Richard Cromonic noted Wonder's influence on the album and praised the creativity of the compositions. He criticized the lyrics as being less creative, and said that *Significant Gains* might be too close to Wonder's sound, calling it "blatant emulation".[[8]](#cite_note-8) The album was not successful, but the song "Baby, I Do Love You" was a minor R&B hit.[[9]](#cite_note-Charts-9) Three years later, Phillinganes released his follow-up album *[Pulse](/wiki/Pulse_(Greg_Phillinganes_album) "Pulse (Greg Phillinganes album)")*, with another minor hit, a cover of [Yellow Magic Orchestra](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra "Yellow Magic Orchestra")'s song "[Behind the Mask](/wiki/Behind_the_Mask_(Yellow_Magic_Orchestra_song) "Behind the Mask (Yellow Magic Orchestra song)")", with additional lyrics by Michael Jackson. This single was more successful on the [dance music charts](/wiki/Hot_Dance_Music/Club_Play "Hot Dance Music/Club Play").[[9]](#cite_note-Charts-9) When he later joined [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton")'s backing band, Phillinganes introduced the tune to Clapton, who covered it on his 1986 *[August](/wiki/August_(Eric_Clapton_album) "August (Eric Clapton album)")* album. Throughout the 1980s and the early 1990s he made solo recordings and toured with Clapton and worked as a session musician for several artists. He continued session work in later years.[[1]](#cite_note-Regen-1)

Phillinganes has worked and toured with musicians including [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison"), the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees"), [Donna Summer](/wiki/Donna_Summer "Donna Summer"), [Anita Baker](/wiki/Anita_Baker "Anita Baker"), [George Benson](/wiki/George_Benson "George Benson"), [Karen Carpenter](/wiki/Karen_Carpenter "Karen Carpenter"), Eric Clapton, [John Mayer](/wiki/John_Mayer "John Mayer"), [Donald Fagen](/wiki/Donald_Fagen "Donald Fagen"), [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), [Patti LaBelle](/wiki/Patti_LaBelle "Patti LaBelle"), Michael Jackson, [Richard Marx](/wiki/Richard_Marx "Richard Marx"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [Al Jarreau](/wiki/Al_Jarreau "Al Jarreau"), [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones"), and [Stevie Nicks](/wiki/Stevie_Nicks "Stevie Nicks").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Beck-11) In 1982, he played on the [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") album *[Hearts and Bones](/wiki/Hearts_and_Bones "Hearts and Bones")* and in 1995, he played on the [Joan Armatrading](/wiki/Joan_Armatrading "Joan Armatrading") album *[What's Inside](/wiki/What%27s_Inside "What's Inside")*.

Starting in 2003, Phillinganes filled in for the semi-retired [David Paich](/wiki/David_Paich "David Paich") on tour with the band [Toto](/wiki/Toto_(band) "Toto (band)"). He became a full-time member of the band by 2005, and contributed to their album *Falling in Between*. He continued to tour as a member of Toto until the band became inactive in 2008, and did not rejoin when Toto re-formed in 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2022, Phillinganes filled in for Toto's Dominique Xavier Taplin during the 'Dogz of Oz' tour during the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway "Norway") shows. Toto announced in 2024 on Facebook that he and drummer [Shannon Forrest](/wiki/Shannon_Forrest "Shannon Forrest") were returning to the touring lineup.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Phillinganes served as the musical director for [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil")'s *[Michael Jackson: The Immortal World Tour](/wiki/Michael_Jackson:_The_Immortal_World_Tour "Michael Jackson: The Immortal World Tour")* for its run from 2011 to 2014.[[4]](#cite_note-atlanta-4) Phillinganes was Musical Director for the 2014 Women of Soul performance at [The White House](/wiki/The_White_House "The White House") with [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), [Patti LaBelle](/wiki/Patti_LaBelle "Patti LaBelle"), [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") and [Jill Scott](/wiki/Jill_Scott_(singer) "Jill Scott (singer)").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2014 and 2015 he performed with Stevie Wonder again in the [Songs in the Key of Life Tour](/wiki/Songs_in_the_Key_of_Life_Tour "Songs in the Key of Life Tour"), also serving "as the tour's Music Director."[[15]](#cite_note-15) He toured with the European leg of [David Gilmour](/wiki/David_Gilmour "David Gilmour")'s [Rattle That Lock](/wiki/Rattle_That_Lock "Rattle That Lock") tour.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
He again toured with Gilmour in 2024 in support of Gilmour's latest album, Luck and Strange.

Phillinganes won a 2015 [Creative Arts Emmy Award](/wiki/Creative_Arts_Emmy_Awards "Creative Arts Emmy Awards") for Outstanding Music Direction for his role as musical director for the television special "Stevie Wonder: Songs In The Key Of Life"[[17]](#cite_note-17) and was music director for the 2016 Grammy Awards.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greg_Phillinganes&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Phillinganes has been married four times. He married his first wife in 1979, after leaving Stevie Wonder's band.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19) He has one daughter, Ellian, with his third wife.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) He is currently married to singer Jory Steinberg with whom he has twin sons, Lennon Gray and Lorne Ellington.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-19)
